sentences.text: Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was not the only thing different about Dora.
Dora was also different in other terms, in that what we had been doing in Dora.
We were to assemble V weapons, the V-1 and V-2, and also began to work on the V-3.
These were the first [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] wonder weapons that Germany promised the nation, from which they believed the war will change.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were the weapons that destroyed a lot of London and other British towns in 1944 and 1945.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The head engineer of these missiles was no other but Wernher von Braun, the same Wernher von Braun who started up our rocket program in the United States.
And he brought with him, when he was brought to the United States, the entire Dora crew, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] some of whom have been deported by now from the United States.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We had a good inkling of what was going on because the Allies were flying overhead, and there was no longer any defense.
They were no longer any German [GERMAN] or any German fighters.
They were just ruling the skies.
And this cooled our hearts.
We looked up and looked at one another and thought, maybe this is it.
Maybe we will be liberated here.
In time, we heard that the front not very far from us, we heard fighting.
We heard shells bursting.
We heard guns.
We heard-- and we hoped that maybe next day, we will be free.
But that was not to be, again.

Our commandant at this time, Max Schmidt, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who took over from Otto Moll when Otto Moll was sent to the east to organize the destruction of Treblinka and Sobibor and the other camps.
He took us out of Dora.

While the other inmates that were in Dora before us were left and were liberated, the 600 or so that we were from Auschwitz, he took us out, put us on barges, on flat barges, on the Elbe, and brought us all the way north to Hamburg.
Yes.
Yes.
It was quite openly talked about.
Some of the electricians and even the workers that we worked with were sabotaging their work.
When we were assigned to a group of Frenchmen, mostly French and Russian-- and some Polish people were there too.
It wasn't per se a strictly Jewish camp.
This was sort of like an international camp.
Some of them, those Russians-- those French people that we worked with, they told us to just take the-- we were supposed to clean parts and put them from one barrel into the other.
And they said, don't clean them.
Just do whatever.
If somebody comes by, try to clean them.
But don't put them in the other barrel.
Put them back in the same barrel.
So the work was obviously not done anymore.
And some of the electricians were-- understand-- I understand, were crossing wires and doing all kind of sabotage.
So from that point on that we came in-- in April there, and very few-- I only saw two or three V weapons leave the [GERMAN], leave the-- the-- the-- the Harz mountains.
What were the conditions like of this camp?
We were in a separate camp, in a-- in a block.
And we received the usual 300, 400 grams of bread in the morning and that at night, and a soup at work.
And a square of marmalade, square of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] margarine.
And that was about the ration that we received in other camps.
Oh.
Well, there were Russians in a separate camp.
And we were not-- we could only meet them at work.
We could only-- we could not interact with them.
We were in separate camp, so to speak.
They were hopeful.
They were already very, very hopeful that it won't be long and the war is over.
Even the foremen were not that zealous anymore.
They were not that convinced that they should go on with their work.
It was kind of a feeling of something is going to happen soon.
What will happen, we don't know.
That was the feeling.
Beg your pardon?
I really didn't meet any Jewish inmates.
We were the first Jewish inmates that came there.
In the caves.
That's where we worked, yes.
There were rails to the outside, on which the V weapons were brought out.
And when they were brought out, we had to lie face down on the ground.
For what reason, I don't know.
Not to tell any-- anybody about it.
Yeah.
Well, Schmidt was a young [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Nazi coming from the Hitler Youth, growing up in a totally [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Nazi Germany, not knowing anything before.
A young man.
He was only about 20 or 21.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And in a sense, from a better family with good manners, and a bit more humane in a sense.
More humane overall than any of the others that we have had.
He came often to the dental station to me, and oftentimes tried to even begin to talk a little bit of his heart.
But he clammed up.
He never did.
But he was kind of conciliatory, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] didn't make us fear him that much.
I took with me some of the most important things that I knew that I would need eventually.
And when we were in Dora-Nordhausen, I just sat in the barrack and I waited for inmates that needed me.
I only had one in the two months that I was there, but I sat there.
I was-- I was assuming that this is my position.
And no one urged me to go into the caves.
I did at first.
But then after a few days, I saw that I can get away with it.
And I-- that's what I did.
Couple of months.
From the end of March to about the end of April.
Perhaps from the middle of March.
About six weeks, I would say.

And when the Americans were coming close, when we heard that the front is progressing to, we were taken out by Max Schmidt, put on barges, set up on the Elbe, and brought to Hamburg, where we were unloaded, fewer than we left, needless to say.
Well, there were a few that were shot on the march from Dora-Nordhausen to the Elbe.
And more so were shot after we left Hamburg to go to his parents' domain, to his parents' large land holding that he had in Neu Glassau.
Why he took us there, no one knew.
Couldn't he just let us go?
He didn't.
The only explanation that we could have is that he feared his own life.
If he would let us go, they would send him to the front, or do something else.
Send him to clean up cities.
And so he kept us.
We were brought in on this large farm of his father's.
And we were given a [GERMAN] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to lay in.
But there wasn't enough room for us.
It was straw in there.
And whoever could squeeze in squeezed in, but the majority of us were laying outside in the cold March and April.
Many lost their lives there, too.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was able to persuade him that I can help the villagers by treating them, and he allowed me to go.
He even gave me the name of one of the villagers to go in and tell him that he send me, and this person allowed me to practice there.
Practice.
To just sit there.
I only had one or two Germans that came in in-- in the whole six weeks.
But the person that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] whose house I was, she organized for me food that I brought back to camp.
Again was able to help my brother and a number of others.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then on May 2, we were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] taken out.
We were very early raised on the [GERMAN] from wherever we slept.
And were taken to Lubeck to a-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --naval station, and brought to the Lubeck Bay, where there were small crafts that took us on and took us out on the ocean.

My brother and I were delivered to a large 28,000 [INAUDIBLE] [? ton ?] German luxury ship called the And were ordered inside the The captain resisted to take us on because he already had 5,000 other inmates in there, and he said he had no room.
But the SS man insisted, and he had to yield.
No.
At this point, Schmidt escaped.
He escaped as just at the time that we were taken to-- to the Bay of Lubeck.
Just a group of SS men.
No one that we knew.
He was German.
Captain Bertram.
He was the captain of the ship in-- in-- in peacetime.
The ship was a luxury ship.
It was traveling between Hamburg and South America, in Brazil and Argentina.
Very luxurious.
Very luxurious.
It was hard to believe that we were put on such luxurious ship.
There was also the and the [GERMAN] nearby within our sight, on which other inmates were already there.
We were just added on to lots of inmates that were inside.
They were from Gross-Rosen, from [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] other camps near Hamburg, nearby.
Stutthof, Gross-Rosen.
And such camps that were in that vicinity.
Some of them were as long as two weeks.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they cleaned us out.
They took everything that we had in food from us because we only came, and they were already hungry for two weeks.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were on the ship for no more than 24 hours, as the following morning we felt a huge detonation, huge [GERMAN] of the ship.
And soon felt that we are not standing on even ground in this room that we were-- that we were-- have to hold on to one another or hold onto the wall to walk.
And as we began to smell smoke, we knew that the ship was afire and sinking.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My brother and I and the others that were with me were trying to open the door, but it was impossible.
And it took some time, like an hour and a half or two in which we were dying in there from the smoke.
Till finally someone opened the door and everyone was pushing against the door.
This by itself was torture, to get to the door and be able to get out.
And when we came up both stairs up to the upper deck were completely in fire.
We could neither penetrate one or the other.
And so we ran [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] from one end of the ship to the other trying to find a way to get out.
Till we finally came to a men's room with toilets with about [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] two feet opening on top, the deck.
There were already other inmates there that were being tried to lift out onto the deck by ropes.
And in this panic, in this melee, one wanted to save his life stepped on top of the other, preventing him from being pulled up.
And this only [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] lengthened the time at which people could be saved.
And finally I was pulled up, and I helped my brother by leaning down.
Somebody held on to my legs.
Pulled him up.
I think it didn't take long after that, and the fire reached-- the smoke reached that room, and whoever was still down there was gone.
No one ever came up again.
I would say, Louise, out of the 6,000 or 7,000 that were on the [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 90% did not make it to deck.
Because on deck we were only about 500, 600, 700.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And there we saw how the ship is burning, and from time to time explosives were bursting and ripping off the deck.
We didn't know where we are safe till we finally came to the edge of the ship and held on to one of the stanchions of the railing with our feet in between, our legs in between.
And waited.
And looked down as people were swimming, trying to swim.
It was all in vain.
They couldn't make it.
The ship was sinking.
And there was an under draft which kept on taking them back and back and back.
It was still three and a half kilometers to shore.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we knew-- my brother was sitting next to me-- we knew that going into the water and trying to make it would never do.
But we also knew that sitting on the ship meant certain death.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At one point, somebody came-- who knew me-- with a rope, and tied it onto one of the stanchions of the railings and asked me to come with him, to lower ourself.
And I said, what good will it do?
He says, well, maybe we will be able to wait it out in the water.
And if a boat comes, or somebody comes to help us, we'll be down there.
We'll be much closer than those that are on deck.
I thought it made a great deal sense.
I thought it made sense.
And so I went to my brother and I ask him to join us.
But he didn't know how to swim.
And so [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were each to our own fate, left to whatever we could do to survive, to rescue.
I lowered myself with this friend of mine.
And as I lowered myself, others seeing this also lowered themself.
And by the time there was seven or eight or nine or 10, the rope unwound and broke.
And we all were in the deep of the North Sea.
Many of us didn't come up more than once or twice, just to gulp a-- a bit of air and went down again.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I held on to the side of the boat with my hands and my knees, and crawled-- I knew that I could not swim away from here.
Until I went to the back of the boat where the draft wasn't.
And at that point, I saw a piece of deck that was swimming several feet from me.
And I said to myself, that's where I am going to swim over to.
And I'm going to put it under my chest.
And it's either going to go down with me, or I survive it-- or will survive with me.
And so I swam over and I started to-- I put it under my chest.
And I started to swim away, paddle away with my legs and feet as much as I could.
Until I went just about, I would say, about a half-- half the length of a football field.
About 50 yards.
And there, I saw a small boat crossing from the northeast to the southwest.
Small boat.
It was only a little point at first.
And then I tried with all my power to get closer, that I can intercept it.
And I almost did, except I was still away about 100 feet.
And I begged them.
I said, please take me on.
Oh, we have no more room.
We can't take anybody on.
There were 10 or 12 naked inmates already in the boat.
And I was naked too, because I left all my things go.
And suddenly, somebody yelled out, this is our dentist.
Let's take him.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
How many miracles did one need to survive?
And sometime, miracles-- sometime you need it more even than miracles.
This was more than a miracle.

And so they pulled me in, and I lay there in pain because my last relative-- my brother, to whom I was very close, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and who was very close to me-- I thought I'll never see again.
The fishermen brought us to the shore, and we stepped out on the sand.
And we looked around and assessed our chances.
We're going to be caught and brought to another concentration camp.
Worse even, brought on another ship or even shot.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] We asked the fishermen where we should go.
Oh, he says.
Go down a little bit up the hill.
You'll see a house.
There's a bakery.
You might find some bread there.
I think, he says, the owner of the bakery probably left.
But you may find at least something to house you.
We were naked.
It was 5 o'clock in the afternoon by the time I was in shore.
We walked, and we saw inmates on the shore, washed ashore dead.
We looked back and we saw the struggle that heroically they were trying to save themself, and it didn't help.
They were disappearing just like bubbles under water, one after the other.
At some point near the ship it looked like as if you had been in a huge rally.
And somebody would photograph from a helicopter, and see only heads and people.
That's how it looked.
And they disappeared one after the other.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We reached this house and went inside.
There was no-- no warm oven.
It was cold.
There was no food.
There were a couple of blankets, which we shared, and laid down and waited, what our fate has for us.
We were too exhausted, too weak, to think even what might happen.
What happen will happen.
We just saved ourself from this catastrophe.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And so the night went on.
And we saw stragglers, one or sometimes two came in.
Fewer and fewer as morning came were able to come-- to survive and come.
Until a truck pulled up, a civilian truck.
And a person came in to tell us that he is taking us from here to a hospital.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we said to him, you're taking us to a camp, aren't you?
You're taking us to the ship?
He said, no.
Don't you know the British are here?
You are free.
And he says, if you don't believe me, go outside and ask my friend.
Well, he was so convincing, and what else could we do?
We went naked as we were on the truck.
And he drove us through the whole town of Neustadt.
At 8:00 in the morning, by that time, naked, 70, 80, 90 people naked.
We covered our private parts with our hands as best as we could.
And brought us to a hospital with white linen sheets, with white covers, and with normal people to address us.
As you will get something to eat soon.
Please take your bed and rest.
And take our temperatures.
And care for us.
It was like we were a different people, all of a sudden.
We had value.
We were human, as we should have been from our date of birth.
This was May 4, 1945.
Just five days before war's end where more than 15,000 inmates who have gone through hell twice, three, four, five times, went to the bottom of the Nazi.
British RAF planes, believe it or not.
They came across the channel, and obviously seeing three German ships, they decided to unload their cargo on us.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Why?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know.
Did they know who we were?
I don't know.
Should they have known who we were?
Perhaps.
But still, I don't know.
I'm asked this question very often, but I have no answer.
We have to stop this tape here.
We'll continue the next tape shortly.
